blocks.x: A smoothie owner keeps the flavor steady by using the same comparison every time: three mango chunks for every two yogurt scoops. That balance between two quantities is a ratio, and it holds whether she is making one cup or filling a pitcher.
A trail stand fills each bag with 5 almonds and 3 dried cherries. A customer orders 4 scoops. How many dried cherries are in the bag?
hints: Every scoop is identical, and the cherries from one scoop do not depend on the almonds at all. Just count the cherries one scoop brings, then ask how many scoops there are.
blocks.sol: The almonds are along for the ride here. What matters is that every scoop is identical, so the cherries simply repeat, three of them at a time. One scoop brings \(3\) cherries. Four scoops bring four copies of that same group, which is \(4\) groups of \(3\). So the count is \(4 \times 3 = 12\). Notice we never had to touch the almonds to answer this, the cherry side of the pairing carries its own steady count. The bag holds \(\boxed{12}\) dried cherries.

blocks.x: At a kite festival the rule is 3 yellow kites for every 5 red ones. One cluster has 9 yellow kites. How many red kites are in the cluster?
hints: The phrase for every 3 yellow there are 5 red is a ratio of \(3:5\). The yellow part grew from \(3\) to \(9\). How many times bigger is that? That same multiplier has to act on the red part too, or the comparison would change.
To go from \(3\) yellow to \(9\) yellow you multiply by \(3\), since \(3\times 3 = 9\). Apply the very same \(\times 3\) to the \(5\) reds.
blocks.sol: The rule for every \(3\) yellow there are \(5\) red is the ratio \(3:5\), and a ratio works much like a fraction in simplest form. As long as you scale both parts by the same number, the comparison stays exactly the same. The yellow part has grown from \(3\) to \(9\). To find the multiplier, ask what \(3\) was multiplied by to reach \(9\). Since \(3\times 3 = 9\), the multiplier is \(3\), which means there are \(3\) groups of the for every unit. Now apply that same \(\times 3\) to the red part, because both colors rise together. That gives \(5\times 3 = 15\) red kites. You can sanity check it. The scaled comparison \(9:15\) reduces back to \(3:5\), so the ratio held steady. \(\boxed{15}\)

blocks.x: A ratio compares two amounts by their relative size. Writing \(3:2\) means for every \(3\) of the first there are \(2\) of the second, and that pairing holds no matter how big the batch grows. On its own a ratio never tells you how many there are altogether, since \(3:2\) could mean \(3\) and \(2\) or \(30\) and \(20\). The same comparison can be written three ways, as \(3:2\) with a colon, as "3 to 2" in words, or as the fraction \(\tfrac{3}{2}\), and the colon form is the one you will reach for most. Order carries meaning, so mango to yogurt is a different comparison than yogurt to mango.

blocks.cap: Each group repeats the exact same pairing, three mango sitting above two yogurt. Line up one group or ten, the blend never changes, so the comparison stays \(3:2\). We can say it in words as three to two or write it as the fraction \(\tfrac{3}{2}\), but most often a ratio is written with a colon as \(3:2\).

blocks.x: A garden plants basil and mint in the ratio \(4:5\). One full repeat of the pair uses \(4+5=9\) plants, so every valid total is a multiple of \(9\). What is the only possible total between 30 and 40?
hints: One full repeat of the comparison is \(4\) basil and \(5\) mint, so each bundle is \(4+5=9\) plants. The real total has to be made of whole bundles, so it must be a multiple of \(9\).
List the multiples of \(9\) and find the one that lands between \(30\) and \(40\). You have \(9, 18, 27, 36, 45\). Which single one fits?
blocks.sol: Each full repeat of the ratio brings \(4\) basil and \(5\) mint, a bundle of \(4+5=9\) plants. Since the garden is built from whole bundles, the total number of plants must be a multiple of \(9\). The multiples of \(9\) are \(9, 18, 27, 36, 45,\) and so on. The only one that sits between \(30\) and \(40\) is \(36\), which is exactly \(4\) bundles. Check it. Four bundles give \(4\times 4=16\) basil and \(4\times 5=20\) mint, and \(16+20=36\), still in the comparison \(4:5\). So the only possible total is \(\boxed{36}\).

blocks.x: One ratio fits a whole family of totals. If the comparison is \(a:b\), the real counts could be \(a\) and \(b\), or twice that, or any matched scale-up, so the ratio alone never says how many things there are in all. What it does pin down is the size of one full repeat, which is \(a+b\), and that is why every honest total lands on a whole number of those repeats. To turn a ratio into actual amounts you need one extra fact, either the total or one of the two quantities.
A cooling fluid should compare water to coolant as \(2:3\). Six ratios were recorded: \(4:6\), \(8:10\), \(6:9\), \(9:12\), \(10:15\), \(14:21\). How many are equivalent to \(2:3\)?
hints: Test each ratio by dividing both numbers by their greatest common factor, the same move you used to reduce a fraction to lowest terms. Then see whether what is left is \(2:3\).

Try \(4:6\). Both numbers divide by \(2\), giving \(2:3\), so that one matches. Now do the same simplifying step to \(8:10\) and notice it becomes \(4:5\), which is not \(2:3\). Work through all six and keep a running count of the ones that land on \(2:3\).
blocks.sol: Reduce each ratio to its simplest form and compare it with \(2:3\). For \(4:6\), both numbers divide by \(2\), giving \(2:3\). Match. For \(8:10\), both divide by \(2\), giving \(4:5\). Not a match. For \(6:9\), both divide by \(3\), giving \(2:3\). Match. For \(9:12\), both divide by \(3\), giving \(3:4\). Not a match. For \(10:15\), both divide by \(5\), giving \(2:3\). Match. For \(14:21\), both divide by \(7\), giving \(2:3\). Match. The ratios \(4:6\), \(6:9\), \(10:15\), and \(14:21\) all reduce to \(2:3\), while \(8:10\) and \(9:12\) do not. That gives \(\boxed{4}\) matching ratios.

blocks.x: Equivalent ratios. A ratio acts like a fraction, so multiplying both parts of \(a:b\) by the same number gives an equivalent ratio, the very same comparison in bigger numbers, the way \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{8}\) are the same amount. Running that backward, dividing both parts by a factor they share keeps the comparison the same too. Dividing by their greatest common factor, as far as it will go, writes the ratio in simplest form, which is reached when the two parts share no common factor larger than \(1\).
A board game ships tokens in the ratio \(18:24\). Reduce it to lowest terms. Write your answer as a:b.
hints: List the factors that \(18\) and \(24\) share. Both are even, so \(2\) works, and both are divisible by \(3\), so \(6\) divides each. Is there anything bigger than \(6\) that divides both?

The greatest common factor is \(6\). Divide each part by \(6\): \(18\div 6\) gives the new first number and \(24\div 6\) gives the new second number. Then check the two results share no common factor larger than \(1\).
blocks.sol: Treat the ratio like a fraction you want in simplest form. First find the greatest common factor of the two parts. The factors of \(18\) are \(1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18\), and the factors of \(24\) are \(1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24\). The largest number in both lists is \(6\), so the greatest common factor is \(6\). Now divide each part by \(6\). For the red tokens, \(18\div 6 = 3\). For the blue tokens, \(24\div 6 = 4\). That turns \(18:24\) into \(3:4\). Finally, check that you are truly finished. The only common factor of \(3\) and \(4\) is \(1\), so nothing larger divides both and the ratio cannot shrink any further. The simplest form of the comparison is \(\boxed{3:4}\).

blocks.x: A school library returned books on time to late in the ratio \(45:75\). Reduce to simplest form. Write as a:b.
hints: Simplifying a ratio works the same way as simplifying a fraction. You want the greatest common factor of \(45\) and \(75\), the biggest number that divides both cleanly. Both end in a \(5\) or a \(0\), so \(5\) divides each, but check whether something larger does too.

Both numbers are multiples of \(15\), since \(45 = 3 \times 15\) and \(75 = 5 \times 15\). Divide each part of the ratio by \(15\), then check that the two results have no common factor left bigger than \(1\).
blocks.sol: The trick is to find the greatest common factor of \(45\) and \(75\), the largest number that divides both with nothing left over. It is tempting to stop at \(5\) since both numbers end in a \(5\), but you can do better. List what fits into each. The number \(45\) breaks into \(3 \times 3 \times 5\), and \(75\) breaks into \(3 \times 5 \times 5\). They share one \(3\) and one \(5\), so their greatest common factor is \(3 \times 5 = 15\). Now divide both parts of the ratio by \(15\). On time becomes \(45 \div 15 = 3\), and late becomes \(75 \div 15 = 5\). That gives \(3:5\). Finally, confirm it is fully reduced. The only number that divides both \(3\) and \(5\) is \(1\), so there is nothing left to cancel. The library's month came down to a clean \(\boxed{3:5}\), three on time for every five late.

blocks.cap: Multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number slides you up and down one family of equivalent ratios, so \(3:4\), \(6:8\), and \(18:24\) all name the same comparison. Climbing up means scaling both parts, and climbing down means splitting them. Dividing both parts of \(18:24\) by their greatest common factor \(6\) brings you all the way down to \(3:4\), the simplest form, where the two parts share no common factor larger than \(1\).

blocks.x: A bakery uses \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) cup almond flour and \(\tfrac{4}{9}\) cup tapioca starch. Write the ratio in lowest terms as a:b.
hints: Both fractions sit over a denominator that divides into \(9\). Multiply each part of the ratio by \(9\). Multiplying both parts by the same number gives an equivalent ratio, the same way \(\tfrac{6}{4}\) and \(\tfrac{3}{2}\) name the same fraction.
After scaling you get \(6:4\). Now finish like you would simplify a fraction. Divide both parts by the largest number that goes into each.
blocks.sol: Start with the ratio of almond flour to tapioca starch, which is \(\tfrac{2}{3}:\tfrac{4}{9}\). Fractions make a ratio hard to read, so clear them first. Multiplying both parts of a ratio by the same number gives an equivalent ratio, and the number \(9\) works for both denominators. $$\tfrac{2}{3}\times 9 = 6 \qquad \tfrac{4}{9}\times 9 = 4$$ So \(\tfrac{2}{3}:\tfrac{4}{9}\) is the same comparison as \(6:4\). Now reduce, exactly like putting a fraction in simplest form. Both parts share a common factor of \(2\), so divide each part by \(2\). $$6\div 2 = 3 \qquad 4\div 2 = 2$$ That leaves \(3:2\), and since \(3\) and \(2\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), it is fully reduced. For every \(3\) parts of almond flour the blend has \(2\) parts of tapioca starch. \(\boxed{3:2}\)

blocks.x: Clear, then reduce. If a ratio shows up wearing fractions, multiply every part by a common denominator to turn them into whole numbers. If it shows up wearing decimals, multiply by a power of ten. That scaling is safe because stretching both parts by the same factor never changes what they are comparing, exactly like building equivalent fractions. Then divide both whole numbers by their greatest common factor to land in lowest terms. Clear first, then reduce.
A trail map records two legs as \(0.75\,\text{km}\) and \(1.25\,\text{km}\). Write \(0.75:1.25\) as a whole-number ratio in lowest terms, in the form a:b.
hints: The decimals reach the hundredths place, so multiply both parts by \(100\). That turns \(0.75\) into \(75\) and \(1.25\) into \(125\), giving the whole-number ratio \(75:125\). Stretching both legs by the same factor of \(100\) keeps the comparison identical, the same way \(\tfrac{75}{125}\) and \(\tfrac{0.75}{1.25}\) are equal fractions.

Now reduce \(75:125\) the way you reduced fractions in Chapter 4. The largest number that divides both \(75\) and \(125\) is \(25\). Divide each part by \(25\) and see what whole-number ratio is left.
blocks.sol: Start with the decimal ratio \(0.75:1.25\). Both numbers stop at the hundredths place, so multiplying each part by \(100\) clears the decimals. $$0.75:1.25 = (0.75\times 100):(1.25\times 100) = 75:125$$ Multiplying both parts by the same number does not change the comparison, just like scaling the top and bottom of a fraction by \(100\) leaves its value alone, so \(75:125\) describes the very same trail. Now reduce \(75:125\) to lowest terms. The greatest common factor of \(75\) and \(125\) is \(25\), so divide each part by \(25\). $$75:125 = (75\div 25):(125\div 25) = 3:5$$ Since \(3\) and \(5\) share no factor larger than \(1\), the ratio is fully reduced. The two legs compare as \(\boxed{3:5}\).

blocks.x: A screen\'s aspect ratio is width to height: \(16:9\) for modern TVs, \(4:3\) for older monitors. Scaling \(4:3\) to \(16:12\) shows \(16:9\) is shorter and wider. One ratio covers every screen size.

Turn a ratio into a count. So far a ratio has fixed only the relative sizes, never the totals. The move that unlocks real amounts is to read a ratio as a recipe built from equal parts, \(a\) parts of one thing and \(b\) parts of the other, every part the same size. Once you find what a single part is worth, every quantity in the story drops out by simple multiplication.
A bag holds 56 pieces of dried fruit mixed as raisins to pumpkin seeds in the ratio \(5:3\). How many pumpkin seeds are in the bag?
hints: The ratio \(5:3\) means the bag is built from \(5\) equal parts of raisins and \(3\) equal parts of seeds. Add those up to see how many equal parts make the whole bag.
There are \(5+3=8\) equal parts in all, and they must total \(56\) pieces. So one part is \(56\div 8\). The seeds are the \(3\) parts, so multiply that one part value by \(3\).
blocks.sol: The ratio \(5:3\) tells you the bag is made of \(5\) equal parts of raisins and \(3\) equal parts of pumpkin seeds. Notice that the ratio by itself does not tell you the totals, it only tells you how the pieces share out. To bring in the real count, add the parts. There are \(5+3=8\) equal parts, and all \(8\) of them together must add up to the whole bag of \(56\) pieces. So each part is worth $$56 \div 8 = 7 \text{ pieces.}$$ Now scale each side by that value. The raisins are \(5\) parts, which is \(5\times 7 = 35\) pieces, and the pumpkin seeds are \(3\) parts, which is \(3\times 7 = 21\) pieces. As a quick check, \(35+21=56\), exactly the size of the bag, and \(35:21\) reduces back to \(5:3\). The bag holds \(\boxed{21}\) pumpkin seeds.

blocks.x: The parts method. Read \(a:b\) as a pile of \(a+b\) equal parts. If you know the total, divide it by \(a+b\) to find what one part is worth, then hand \(a\) of those parts to one side and \(b\) to the other to get the real amounts. If instead you know one quantity, divide it by its part count to find the value of one part, then scale the other side by that same multiplier. Once you know what one part is, the whole comparison falls open.

blocks.cap: A ratio is really just a count of equal parts. The \(5:3\) ratio cuts the snack mix into \(5+3=8\) equal parts, and since those parts have to add up to the whole batch, each part is worth \(56\div 8=7\) pieces. Once you know one part, you scale each side to its real amount, \(5\times 7=35\) raisins and \(3\times 7=21\) seeds, and sure enough \(35+21=56\). This is the parts method, find the value of one part, then hand it to both sides of the ratio.

blocks.x: A box of 84 stickers is split between two friends in the ratio \(5:7\), bigger share to the friend who paid more. How many stickers are in the smaller share?
hints: Add the two numbers in the ratio to find how many equal parts the box is divided into. Then figure out how many stickers sit in a single part.
One part is \(84 \div 12 = 7\) stickers. The smaller share is the \(5\)-part side, so it holds five of those parts.
blocks.sol: The ratio \(5:7\) tells you the box is shared in \(5 + 7 = 12\) equal parts. To find the size of one part, divide the total by the number of parts, so \(84 \div 12 = 7\) stickers per part. The smaller share is the \(5\)-part side, which is \(5 \times 7 = 35\) stickers. As a quick check, the larger share is \(7 \times 7 = 49\), and \(35 + 49 = 84\), the whole box. The smaller share holds \(\boxed{35}\) stickers.

blocks.x: A lemonade recipe uses lemon juice to water in the ratio \(2:9\). One batch uses 6 ounces of lemon juice. How many ounces does the whole batch hold?
hints: The lemon side of the ratio is \(2\) parts, and those \(2\) parts landed on six ounces. What number do you multiply \(2\) by to get \(6\)? That single number is the multiplier for every part of the ratio.
One full repeat of the recipe is \(2 + 9 = 11\) parts. Multiply those \(11\) parts by the multiplier you found, and you have the whole batch in ounces.
blocks.sol: Start from the side you actually know. The lemon juice is \(2\) parts in the ratio, and in this batch those \(2\) parts come out to six ounces. So each single part must be worth \(6 \div 2 = 3\) ounces. That number \(3\) is the multiplier, the amount one part is really worth. Now scale the whole recipe by it. One complete repeat of the ratio is \(2 + 9 = 11\) parts, so the finished batch is \(11 \times 3 = 33\) ounces. You can check it by parts. The lemon juice is \(2 \times 3 = 6\) ounces, the water is \(9 \times 3 = 27\) ounces, and \(6 + 27 = 33\) ounces, which matches. The whole batch is \(\boxed{33}\) ounces.

blocks.x: An aquarium opens with 40 fish, blue to green in \(3:2\). The keeper adds only green fish until the ratio reads \(6:5\). How many green fish must the keeper add?
hints: The blue total never moves, it stays at \(24\). So your job is to find how much green pairs with \(24\) blue when the comparison is \(6:5\), then compare that to the \(16\) green already swimming there.

In the new comparison \(6:5\), the blue is \(6\) parts and equals \(24\) fish, so one part is \(24\div 6=4\). The green is \(5\) parts. Find the green count, then subtract the \(16\) you started with.
blocks.sol: Start by turning the opening ratio into real counts. The ratio \(3:2\) means \(3+2=5\) parts, and \(40\div 5=8\) fish per part, so there are \(3\times 8=24\) blue and \(2\times 8=16\) green. Only green fish get added, so the blue stays anchored at \(24\) forever. Now read the target comparison \(6:5\) the same way. Blue is the \(6\) side and it equals \(24\) fish, so one part is \(24\div 6=4\) fish. The green side is \(5\) parts, which is \(5\times 4=20\) green fish. The tank already holds \(16\) green, so the keeper needs \(20-16=4\) more. Check it, with \(24\) blue and \(20\) green the comparison is \(24:20\), and dividing both by \(4\) gives \(6:5\), exactly the goal. So the keeper must add \(\boxed{4}\) green fish.

review.x: A vendor packs gift bags with 4 glitter stickers and 6 mini erasers each. She fills 5 bags. How many mini erasers does she use in total?
hints: The ratio inside one bag pairs the stickers and erasers, but here you only need the eraser part of it. Each bag holds the same number of mini erasers, so figure out how many bags share that count.
Take the number of erasers in one bag and add it once for every bag she fills. That is five copies of six.
review.sol: A ratio inside one bag pairs the stickers and erasers, but this question asks only about the erasers, so the sticker count is a distraction. Each bag holds the same six mini erasers, and she fills five identical bags. Five bags each carrying six erasers gives \(5 \times 6 = 30\) erasers in all. So she uses \(\boxed{30}\) mini erasers across the five bags.

review.x: A swim club keeps 2 coaches for every 9 swimmers. This morning 36 swimmers arrived. How many coaches are on deck?
hints: The comparison is \(2:9\). Ask yourself how many of those nine swimmer units fit inside \(36\) swimmers.
Since \(36 = 9 \times 4\), the unit repeats four times, so the coaches repeat four times too. Multiply \(2\) by that same \(4\).
review.sol: The staffing is fixed at \(2:9\), two coaches for every nine swimmers. To keep the comparison the same when more people show up, both parts of the ratio scale by the same multiplier. The swimmers went from \(9\) to \(36\), and \(36 \div 9 = 4\), so the for every unit repeats four times. The coaches must repeat four times as well, which gives \(2 \times 4 = 8\) coaches. As a check, \(8:36\) reduces by dividing both parts by \(4\), and \(8 \div 4 = 2\) with \(36 \div 4 = 9\), so \(8:36\) is the same comparison as \(2:9\). There are \(\boxed{8}\) coaches on deck.

review.x: A flower stand bundles tulips and daffodils in the ratio \(5:6\). One full repeat is \(5+6=11\) stems, so every valid total is a multiple of 11. What is the only total between 70 and 80?
hints: A working total must split cleanly into groups of \(11\) stems, so it has to be a multiple of \(11\). Which multiples of \(11\) sit between \(70\) and \(80\)?
List the multiples of \(11\) near that range. \(11\times 6=66\) is too small and \(11\times 8=88\) is too big, so look at \(11\times 7\).
review.sol: Each full repeat of the \(5:6\) comparison uses \(5+6=11\) stems, so any real total must be made of complete groups of \(11\). That means the total has to be a multiple of \(11\). Checking the multiples of \(11\) near the range, \(11\times 6=66\) is below \(70\) and \(11\times 8=88\) is above \(80\), while \(11\times 7=77\) lands neatly between them. So the only total that splits evenly into \(5:6\) bundles here is \(77\), which gives \(7\) repeats, meaning \(35\) tulips and \(42\) daffodils. The single possible total is \(\boxed{77}\).

review.x: A fabric border repeats wide stripes to narrow stripes in the ratio \(9:12\). Reduce first, then scale up so the wide count reaches 24. How many narrow stripes go with 24 wide ones?
hints: Reduce \(9:12\) first by dividing both parts by their common factor. Both \(9\) and \(12\) share a factor of \(3\), so the comparison becomes \(3:4\).
From \(3\) wide stripes to \(24\) wide stripes you multiplied by \(8\), since \(3\times 8 = 24\). Multiply the narrow part by that same \(8\).
review.sol:
Start by reducing the comparison. Both parts of \(9:12\) share a common factor of \(3\), so dividing each part gives \(\tfrac{9}{3}:\tfrac{12}{3}\), which is \(3:4\). That tells you the simplest pattern is \(3\) wide stripes for every \(4\) narrow ones.

Now scale up to reach \(24\) wide stripes. To turn the \(3\) wide stripes into \(24\), multiply by \(8\), since \(3\times 8 = 24\). To keep the comparison the same, multiply the narrow part by that very same factor, so \(4\times 8 = 32\).

You can check it against the original numbers too. Going from \(9\) wide to \(24\) wide is a factor of \(\tfrac{24}{9} = \tfrac{8}{3}\), and \(12\times \tfrac{8}{3} = 32\) as well. Either path lands in the same place. So \(24\) wide stripes go with \(\boxed{32}\) narrow stripes.

review.x: A juice bar restocks mango and guava pods in the ratio \(24:36\). Simplify to lowest terms. Write your answer as a:b.
hints: Look for the greatest common factor of \(24\) and \(36\). What is the biggest number that divides evenly into both?
Both \(24\) and \(36\) are divisible by \(12\). Divide each part by \(12\) and then check that the two new numbers have no shared factor left.
review.sol: A ratio behaves like a fraction, so simplifying it works the same way you reduced fractions in Chapter 4. You divide both parts by the same number until nothing is left to cancel. The fastest route is to divide by the greatest common factor of the two parts at once. The greatest common factor of \(24\) and \(36\) is \(12\), since \(12\) divides both and no larger number does. Dividing each part by \(12\) gives \(24 \div 12 = 2\) and \(36 \div 12 = 3\), so the ratio becomes \(2:3\). Now check that it is fully reduced. The only positive factor shared by \(2\) and \(3\) is \(1\), so there is nothing left to cancel and the ratio is in lowest terms. The simplified ratio is \(\boxed{2:3}\).

review.x: A bakery sells sourdough to rye in the ratio \(42:54\). Simplify to lowest terms. Write your answer as a:b.
hints: A ratio simplifies just like a fraction. Find the largest number that divides both \(42\) and \(54\) evenly, then divide each part by it.
Both \(42\) and \(54\) are even, and both are divisible by \(3\), so they share a factor of \(6\). Divide each side by \(6\).
review.sol: A ratio behaves like a fraction, so simplest form means the two parts share no common factor larger than \(1\). Start by finding the greatest common factor of \(42\) and \(54\). The factors they share are \(2\) and \(3\), and \(2\times 3=6\), so the greatest common factor is \(6\). Now divide each part of the ratio by \(6\). For the sourdough, \(42\div 6=7\), and for the rye, \(54\div 6=9\). That gives the simplified ratio \(7:9\). Since \(7\) and \(9\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. So the bakery sells sourdough and rye in the ratio \(\boxed{7:9}\).

review.x: A smoothie uses \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) cup berries and \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) cup banana. Clear the fractions and reduce to lowest terms. Write the ratio of berries to banana as a:b.
hints: The two amounts are \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{9}\). A number that both \(6\) and \(9\) divide into evenly is \(18\), so try multiplying both parts of the ratio by \(18\).
Multiplying turns \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) into \(15\) and \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) into \(10\), so the ratio becomes \(15:10\). Now divide both numbers by their greatest common factor to finish.
review.sol: The two amounts being compared are \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) cup of berries and \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) cup of banana, so the ratio is \(\tfrac{5}{6}:\tfrac{5}{9}\). A ratio does not change when you multiply both parts by the same number, just like an equivalent fraction. Pick a number that clears both denominators. Both \(6\) and \(9\) divide evenly into \(18\), so multiply each part by \(18\). The berries part becomes \(\tfrac{5}{6}\times 18 = 15\), and the banana part becomes \(\tfrac{5}{9}\times 18 = 10\). That gives the whole-number ratio \(15:10\). Now reduce it. The greatest common factor of \(15\) and \(10\) is \(5\), so divide both by \(5\) to get \(15\div 5 = 3\) and \(10\div 5 = 2\). The ratio in lowest terms is \(\boxed{3:2}\).

review.x: A window glaze uses \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) cup fast-drying liquid and \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) cup slow-drying liquid. Multiply both by 8, then reduce. Write the ratio in the form a:b.
hints: Multiplying both parts of a ratio by the same number gives an equivalent ratio. Try multiplying each amount by \(8\) and see what whole numbers you get.
After scaling, you have \(4\) to \(3\). Check whether \(4\) and \(3\) share any common factor bigger than \(1\).
review.sol: The two amounts are \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) cup and \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) cup, so the ratio is \(\tfrac{1}{2} : \tfrac{3}{8}\). Scaling both parts of a ratio by the same number keeps the ratio equivalent, just like building up equivalent fractions. Multiply each part by \(8\). The first part becomes \(\tfrac{1}{2} \times 8 = 4\), and the second becomes \(\tfrac{3}{8} \times 8 = 3\). That turns the comparison into \(4 : 3\). Now check for common factors. The factors of \(4\) are \(1, 2, 4\) and the factors of \(3\) are \(1, 3\), so the only shared factor is \(1\). Since nothing larger divides both, \(4 : 3\) is already in lowest terms. The ratio of fast-drying to slow-drying liquid is \(\boxed{4:3}\).

review.x: Two garden rows measure \(0.6\) m and \(1.5\) m. Rewrite \(0.6:1.5\) as a whole-number ratio in simplest form. Write your answer as a:b.
hints: A ratio behaves like a fraction, so you can multiply both parts by the same number without changing what it means. Both parts have one decimal place, so think about what single multiplier turns each into a whole number.
Multiplying both parts by \(10\) gives \(6:15\). Now find the largest number that divides both \(6\) and \(15\), and divide both parts by it.
review.sol: Start with \(0.6:1.5\). A ratio works the same way a fraction does, so multiplying both parts by the same number keeps the comparison the same. Both numbers have one decimal place, so multiply both parts by \(10\) to clear the decimals. That gives \(6:15\). Now reduce to lowest terms. The largest number that divides both \(6\) and \(15\) is \(3\), so divide both parts by \(3\). You get \(6\div 3 = 2\) and \(15\div 3 = 5\), which is \(2:5\). Since \(2\) and \(5\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. The rows compare in the ratio \(\boxed{2:5}\).

review.x: A soup base uses \(2.4\) cups broth and \(0.9\) cups cream. Multiply by 10 to clear decimals, then reduce. Write the ratio as a:b.
hints: Both parts have a digit in the tenths place, so multiply each part by \(10\). That turns \(2.4:0.9\) into a ratio of two whole numbers.
Once you have \(24:9\), find the greatest common factor of \(24\) and \(9\), then divide both parts by it.
review.sol: Start by clearing the decimals. Multiplying both parts of \(2.4:0.9\) by \(10\) keeps the comparison the same, since scaling both parts by the same amount gives an equivalent ratio. That gives \(24:9\). Now reduce to lowest terms, just like simplifying a fraction. The greatest common factor of \(24\) and \(9\) is \(3\), so divide both parts by \(3\). You get \(24\div 3 = 8\) and \(9\div 3 = 3\), which leaves \(8:3\). Since \(8\) and \(3\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. The recipe holds \(8\) parts broth for every \(3\) parts cream, so the answer is \(\boxed{8:3}\).

review.x: A bracelet has 72 beads, copper to jade in the ratio \(4:5\). How many jade beads are on the bracelet?
hints: Add the two parts of the ratio to see how many equal parts the whole bracelet is cut into, then find what one part is worth.
One part is \(72\div 9 = 8\) beads. Jade is the five-part color, so multiply the value of one part by \(5\).
review.sol: The ratio \(4:5\) tells you copper takes \(4\) parts and jade takes \(5\) parts, so the bracelet is divided into \(4+5=9\) equal parts. Those nine parts share the \(72\) beads evenly, so one part is worth $$72\div 9 = 8 \text{ beads}.$$ Jade is the five-part color, so it gets five of those equal parts, \(5\times 8 = 40\) beads. As a quick check, copper gets \(4\times 8 = 32\) beads, and \(40+32=72\), which matches the whole bracelet. So the number of jade beads is \(\boxed{40}\).

review.x: A 90-minute playlist splits jazz to folk in the ratio \(7:3\). The jazz piece is the larger share. How many minutes long is the jazz piece?
hints: The ratio \(7:3\) hides the total. Add the two numbers in the ratio to see how many equal parts the playlist is cut into, then share the ninety minutes among them.
First find the value of one part by dividing, \(90 \div 10 = 9\) minutes. The jazz piece is seven of those parts, so multiply \(7\) by the value of one part.
review.sol: The ratio \(7:3\) tells you the playlist is split into \(7 + 3 = 10\) equal parts, with jazz getting \(7\) of them and folk getting \(3\). Find the value of one part by sharing the ninety minutes among the ten parts, \(90 \div 10 = 9\) minutes per part. The jazz piece is seven parts, so \(7 \times 9 = 63\) minutes. As a quick check, folk gets \(3 \times 9 = 27\) minutes, and \(63 + 27 = 90\), the whole playlist. The jazz piece is \(\boxed{63}\) minutes.

review.x: A potting soil blend mixes compost to sand in the ratio \(3:8\). One batch uses 15 kg of compost, where one part equals one kilogram. What is the total mass of the batch?
hints: The compost is the 3 part of the ratio, and it weighs 15 kilograms. What number do you multiply 3 by to reach 15? That same multiplier scales every part of the mix.
One full repeat of the mix is \(3 + 8 = 11\) kilograms. Multiply that by the multiplier you found to get the whole batch.
review.sol: The ratio \(3:8\) means compost takes \(3\) parts and sand takes \(8\) parts, so one full repeat of the mix is \(3 + 8 = 11\) kilograms. The compost is the \(3\) part, and here it actually weighs \(15\) kilograms. To find the multiplier, ask what \(3\) is multiplied by to give \(15\). That is \(15 \div 3 = 5\), so the real batch is \(5\) copies of the basic mix. Now scale the whole mix by that same multiplier. One repeat weighs \(11\) kilograms, so the full batch is \(11 \times 5 = 55\) kilograms. You can check it by parts. Compost is \(3 \times 5 = 15\) kilograms and sand is \(8 \times 5 = 40\) kilograms, and \(15 + 40 = 55\) kilograms. The total mass of the batch is \(\boxed{55}\).

review.x: A reef tank holds 35 fish in the ratio \(3:4\) blue to gold. The keeper adds only blue fish until the count is \(1:1\). How many blue fish must she add?
hints: The ratio \(3:4\) splits the tank into \(3+4=7\) equal parts. Divide the thirty five fish among those parts to find the value of one part, then count the blue and the gold fish you begin with.
Adding only blue fish leaves the gold count frozen. A \(1:1\) split means blue must rise until it equals the gold count. Subtract the starting blue count from that target to see how many you tack on.
review.sol: The ratio \(3:4\) divides the tank into \(3+4=7\) equal parts. With \(35\) fish in all, one part is \(35\div 7=5\) fish. So the tank starts with \(3\times 5=15\) blue fish and \(4\times 5=20\) gold fish, which checks out since \(15+20=35\). Now only blue fish get added, so the gold count stays locked at \(20\). An even split of \(1:1\) means blue must climb until it matches the gold, so blue needs to reach \(20\). It began at \(15\), so the keeper adds \(20-15=5\) blue fish. \(\boxed{5}\)

blocks.x: A kombucha brewer keeps the same blend of tea, juice, and ginger every batch, writing it \(6:4:1\). Three quantities marching together work exactly like a two-way ratio, same idea, just one more seat at the table.
A garden potting mix follows the ratio \(5:3:2\), compost to peat to sand. Using exactly one unscaled batch, how many scoops of potting mix is that in total?
hints: A multi-way ratio like \(5:3:2\) names the actual amount of each part in one batch. So this batch really is 5 scoops of compost, 3 scoops of peat, and 2 scoops of sand.
Every scoop ends up in the same pile, so the total is just the parts of the ratio added together. Add \(5+3+2\).
blocks.sol: A three-way ratio works the same way a two-way ratio does, it just tracks more parts at once. The ratio \(5:3:2\) tells you that one batch is 5 parts compost, 3 parts peat, and 2 parts sand. Since you are using one unscaled batch, each part is exactly 1 scoop, so the amounts are 5 scoops, 3 scoops, and 2 scoops. Everything goes into the same pile, so the total is the sum of all the parts, \(5+3+2=10\). The whole batch is \(\boxed{10}\) scoops.

blocks.x: A marching band lines up in the ratio \(5:3:1\), brass to woodwinds to percussion. Reading the ratio in that order, how many parts belong to the woodwinds?
hints: The labels are read in the same order as the numbers. The first number goes with brass, the second number goes with woodwinds, the third number goes with percussion. So which number sits in the woodwinds spot?
Line them up. Brass matches the \(5\), woodwinds matches the \(3\), and percussion matches the \(1\). The woodwinds part is the middle number.
blocks.sol: In a multi-way ratio the order is the meaning. Each number is glued to the label in the matching position of the reading. Reading \(5:3:1\) as brass to woodwinds to percussion lines them up cleanly. Brass takes the first part, \(5\). Woodwinds takes the second part, \(3\). Percussion takes the third part, \(1\). The new member only needs the woodwinds spot, and that is the middle number, so the woodwinds share is \(\boxed{3}\) parts.

blocks.x: Multi-way ratios. A ratio does not have to stop at two things. Writing \(a:b:c\) compares three quantities at once, read "a to b to c", where the order is the whole point since it tells you which number belongs to which quantity. Like any ratio it pins down only the relative sizes and never the total on its own. Everything you already know carries straight over, equivalent ratios, simplifying, and the parts method, with simply more parts now and every one of them kept in step.

<svg viewBox="0 0 456 232" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A multi-part bar model for the fizz-punch ratio 6 to 4 to 1. One long horizontal bar is split into 11 equal unit segments by thin borders, so every segment is the same width. The first 6 segments on the left are shaded a pale neutral tone, gathered under a bracket labeled soda water, 6 parts. The next 4 segments in the middle are shaded gold, gathered under a bracket labeled cranberry juice, 4 parts. The single last segment on the right is shaded a deep gold, with a bracket labeled ginger syrup, 1 part. blocks.cap: This is one batch of fizz-punch shown as a single bar cut into \(6+4+1=11\) equal parts. The bar tells you nothing about how big the cup is, only how the three drinks compare, \(6\) parts soda water to \(4\) parts cranberry juice to \(1\) part ginger syrup. You could pour tiny shots or fill a giant bowl, and as long as the parts keep the ratio \(6:4:1\) it tastes the same. The part counts are what stay fixed while the real amounts scale up together.

blocks.x: A florist arranges roses to lilies to ferns in the ratio \(3:2:4\). What is the smallest total number of stems that fits this comparison exactly?
hints: The three numbers \(3\), \(2\), \(4\) tell you the size of each share inside one full set. Add them up to see how many stems a single set of shares uses, then ask whether you could ever use fewer than that and still keep at least one of each.

One full set is \(3\) roses, \(2\) lilies, and \(4\) ferns. Bigger windows just stack more identical sets, so every valid total is a multiple of that one set's size. The smallest total is the size of a single set, which is why \(8\) cannot work.
blocks.sol: A multi-way ratio hides a total, just like a two-way ratio does. The comparison \(3:2:4\) splits the window into \(3+2+4=9\) equal shares, so one full set is \(3\) roses, \(2\) lilies, and \(4\) ferns, which is \(9\) stems. The greatest common factor of \(3\), \(2\), and \(4\) is \(1\), so the comparison is already in lowest terms and you cannot shrink it any further. Every window that fits the comparison is some whole number of these sets, so every valid total is a multiple of \(9\), like \(9\), \(18\), \(27\), and so on. Since \(8\) is less than one full set, there is no way to keep at least one of each kind and still land on \(8\), which is exactly why the florist rules it out. The smallest total that works is a single full set, \(\boxed{9}\).

blocks.x: A multi-way ratio hides a total. When a mix follows \(a:b:c\), the real amounts could be \(a\), \(b\), \(c\), or twice each, or any matched scale-up \(na:nb:nc\), and every one of those is the same comparison. The trick is that the whole triple grows by one shared multiplier, never one part on its own. That is why the total is always a multiple of \(a+b+c\), and why one ratio can show up at many different total sizes.
A muralist mixes 18 oz blue, 12 oz white, and 30 oz black. Find the GCF of all three parts and write the simplified ratio of blue to white to black in the form a:b:c.
hints: With a three-way ratio you reduce the same way you would a two-way one, except the factor has to divide every part at once. Ask what is the largest whole number that goes evenly into \(18\), into \(12\), and into \(30\) all three.

List the factors of each part and hunt for the biggest one they share. \(18\) splits as \(6\times 3\), \(12\) as \(6\times 2\), and \(30\) as \(6\times 5\), so the common factor is \(6\). Divide all three parts by \(6\).
blocks.sol: To simplify a multi-way ratio, divide every part by the greatest common factor that all the parts share. So first find the largest whole number that divides \(18\), \(12\), and \(30\) evenly. Checking the biggest candidates, \(6\) works for all three since \(18 = 6\times 3\), \(12 = 6\times 2\), and \(30 = 6\times 5\), and nothing larger divides all three. So the greatest common factor is \(6\). Now divide each part by \(6\): blue becomes \(18\div 6 = 3\), white becomes \(12\div 6 = 2\), and black becomes \(30\div 6 = 5\). That gives \(3:2:5\), and since \(3\), \(2\), and \(5\) share no common factor beyond \(1\), it is fully reduced. The cleanest form of the recipe is \(\boxed{3:2:5}\).

blocks.x: Reducing a multi-way ratio. Divide every part by the greatest common factor that all the parts share together, not a factor that only fits two of them. It is the same move as reducing a fraction, just spread across more numbers. Watch the trap of staring at one small part and deciding you must be finished, since a part being tiny means nothing on its own. You are done only when no whole number larger than \(1\) divides all the parts at the same time.
A board game ships action, coin, and gem tokens in the ratio \(6:10:15\). Write the ratio in lowest terms as a:b:c.
hints: To simplify a multi-way ratio you cannot just look at two parts at a time. You need a number that divides into every part at once. Check each part: does \(2\) go into \(6\), \(10\), and \(15\)? Does \(3\)? Does \(5\)? A factor only counts if it works for all three.

List the factors of each part. \(6 = 2\times 3\), \(10 = 2\times 5\), \(15 = 3\times 5\). The pairs each share something, but no single number divides all three. So the greatest common factor of all three is \(1\), and dividing every part by \(1\) changes nothing.
blocks.sol: To simplify a multi-way ratio, you divide every part by the greatest common factor of all the parts at once, not just one pair. So break each number into its factors. \(6 = 2\times 3\), \(10 = 2\times 5\), and \(15 = 3\times 5\). Now look for a factor shared by all three. The number \(2\) divides \(6\) and \(10\) but not \(15\). The number \(3\) divides \(6\) and \(15\) but not \(10\). The number \(5\) divides \(10\) and \(15\) but not \(6\). Each pair shares something, yet no single number bigger than \(1\) divides all three. That makes the greatest common factor of \(6\), \(10\), and \(15\) equal to \(1\). Dividing every part by \(1\) leaves the comparison exactly as it was, so the ratio is already in lowest terms. The player's instinct was a trap. A shared factor inside a pair does not mean the whole thing shrinks. The simplified ratio is \(\boxed{6:10:15}\).

blocks.x: A baker\'s blend uses \(1\tfrac{1}{3}\) cups oat flour, \(2\) cups rice flour, and \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) cup almond meal. Clear the fractions and reduce. Write the ratio of oat to rice to almond as a:b:c.
hints: Every part is either a whole number or a number of thirds, so think about what single multiplier would turn all three into whole numbers at once. Multiplying every part by \(3\) does it, because \(1\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{4}{3}\) and \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) both clear when tripled.
After scaling by \(3\) you get \(4:6:2\). Now find the greatest common factor of all three parts and divide every part by it to reach lowest terms.
blocks.sol: First rewrite the awkward part as an improper fraction so the whole blend is in thirds. Oat flour is \(1\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{4}{3}\), rice flour is \(2=\tfrac{6}{3}\), and almond meal is \(\tfrac{2}{3}\). Every part is now a number of thirds, so multiplying all three parts by \(3\) clears the denominators at the same time. That gives \(\tfrac{4}{3}\times 3=4\), \(2\times 3=6\), and \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times 3=2\), so the ratio becomes \(4:6:2\). Scaling every part by the same number keeps the comparison the same, it just dresses it in whole numbers. Now simplify. The greatest common factor of \(4\), \(6\), and \(2\) is \(2\), so divide every part by \(2\) to get \(2:3:1\). Since \(2\), \(3\), and \(1\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. The blend compares oat flour to rice flour to almond meal as \(\boxed{2:3:1}\).

blocks.x: Clear fractions in a multi-way ratio by multiplying every part by a common denominator, so the fractions and mixed numbers all turn into whole numbers at once. Stretching every part by the same factor never changes what they are comparing, so the ratio you get is the same comparison wearing whole numbers. Then finish the usual way, dividing every part by the greatest common factor they all share to reach lowest terms.
A terrarium is layered sand to gravel to soil in the ratio \(3:5:7\), filling 60 scoops total. How many scoops of gravel does the terrarium use?
hints: The ratio \(3:5:7\) means the 60 scoops are shared into \(3+5+7\) equal parts. Add those to find the total number of parts.
There are \(15\) parts in all, so one part is \(60\div 15\) scoops. Gravel is the \(5\) in the ratio, so it gets \(5\) of those parts.
blocks.sol: The ratio \(3:5:7\) tells you the jar is split into \(3+5+7=15\) equal parts. Those \(15\) parts together make up all \(60\) scoops, so one part is worth $$\frac{60}{15}=4 \text{ scoops}.$$ Gravel is the middle number in the ratio, so it takes up \(5\) parts. Five parts at \(4\) scoops each gives \(5\times 4=20\) scoops. (As a check, sand is \(3\times 4=12\) and soil is \(7\times 4=28\), and \(12+20+28=60\), the full jar.) So the gravel uses \(\boxed{20}\) scoops.

blocks.x: Parts method with many shares. Read \(a:b:c\) as one pile of \(a+b+c\) equal parts. Add every number to get the part count, divide the real total by that count to find what one part is worth, then each quantity is its own part count times that one-part value. It is the exact same engine whether there are two, three, or more shares, and the only thing that changes is how many numbers you add up at the start.

blocks.cap: A stained-glass panel of 84 chips split among amber, teal, clear, and rose in the ratio \(5:4:3:2\). The parts method still works with four shares. Add the parts to get \(5+4+3+2=14\), then one part is \(84\div 14=6\) chips. Scale each share to its real count, \(5\times 6=30\), \(4\times 6=24\), \(3\times 6=18\), and \(2\times 6=12\), and they rebuild the whole panel since \(30+24+18+12=84\).

blocks.x: A choir keeps sopranos to altos to tenors in the ratio \(6:5:4\). Tonight 18 sopranos arrived. How many tenors are at the rehearsal?
hints: The sopranos hold the soprano part of the comparison, which is 6. So 18 sopranos fill up 6 equal parts. Divide to find what just one part is worth, then remember the tenors hold 4 of those same parts.
One part is \(18 \div 6 = 3\) singers. Tenors take the \(4\) part, so multiply \(4 \times 3\).
blocks.sol: The comparison \(6:5:4\) splits the choir into \(6+5+4 = 15\) equal parts in all, but you only need the part size to answer this. The sopranos own the first number, so they fill \(6\) of those equal parts. Tonight 18 sopranos walk in, which means those 6 parts together hold 18 singers. One part is therefore \(18 \div 6 = 3\) singers. Every section is measured in these same parts, and the tenors own the \(4\), so the tenors hold \(4\) parts of \(3\) singers each, giving \(4 \times 3 = 12\). A quick sanity check fits the comparison nicely, since \(18\) sopranos, \(15\) altos, and \(12\) tenors reduce straight back to \(6:5:4\). So the number of tenors is \(\boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: An orchard plants apple to pear to plum in the ratio \(4:3:2\). This season there are 24 plum trees. How many trees are in the whole orchard?
hints: The plum trees are the \(2\) part of the ratio \(4:3:2\). So those \(24\) plum trees are spread evenly across \(2\) equal parts. Divide to find what just one part is worth.
One part is \(24 \div 2 = 12\) trees. Now count how many parts the whole orchard has, \(4+3+2\), and multiply that many parts by \(12\).
blocks.sol: The ratio \(4:3:2\) compares apple to pear to plum, so the plum trees sit in the \(2\) part. The grower counted \(24\) plum trees, and those \(24\) trees fill exactly \(2\) equal parts. To find the value of a single part, share the plum count across its parts, \(24 \div 2 = 12\) trees per part. Every part of this ratio is worth that same \(12\) trees. The whole orchard is built from all the parts together, \(4+3+2 = 9\) parts. Multiply the number of parts by the value of one part, \(9 \times 12 = 108\). As a check, apple is \(4 \times 12 = 48\), pear is \(3 \times 12 = 36\), and plum is \(2 \times 12 = 24\), and \(48 + 36 + 24 = 108\). So the whole orchard holds \(\boxed{108}\) trees.

blocks.x: Sometimes you get two overlapping ratios that share one quantity, one tying together this pair of things and a second tying together that pair, with a single quantity sitting in both. You want all three in one clean \(a:b:c\). The trouble is the shared quantity almost never shows up as the same number in the two ratios, so they refuse to line up on sight. The next problems uncover the one move that fixes exactly that and snaps the two ratios into one.
A gear train: front to middle is \(4:3\) and middle to rear is \(2:5\). Rescale so the middle numbers match, then write front to rear as a:b in lowest terms.
hints: The middle gear is counted as \(3\) in the first ratio and \(2\) in the second. To stitch the two ratios together you need the middle number to be the same in both. Pick a common multiple of \(3\) and \(2\), and \(6\) is the smallest one.

Scale \(4:3\) up by \(2\) to get \(8:6\), and scale \(2:5\) up by \(3\) to get \(6:15\). Now both say \(6\) for the middle, so line them up as front to middle to rear and look at just the front and rear numbers.
blocks.sol: The two ratios share the middle gear, but they count it differently. Front to middle is \(4:3\), so the middle shows up as \(3\). Middle to rear is \(2:5\), so the middle shows up as \(2\). Before I can chain them into one front to middle to rear ratio, those middle numbers have to agree. I need a common multiple of \(3\) and \(2\), and the smallest is \(6\). Scale the first ratio up so its middle becomes \(6\). Multiply \(4:3\) by \(2\) to get \(8:6\). Scale the second ratio up so its middle becomes \(6\) too. Multiply \(2:5\) by \(3\) to get \(6:15\). Now both ratios call the middle gear \(6\), so they snap together into one chain, \(8:6:15\), reading front to middle to rear. The front gear is \(8\) and the rear gear is \(15\). Their comparison is \(8:15\), and since \(8\) and \(15\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), it is already in lowest terms. So the front gear to rear gear ratio is \(\boxed{8:15}\).

blocks.x: Linking two ratios through a shared part. When \(A:B\) and \(B:C\) both talk about the same middle quantity \(B\) but print different numbers for it, scale each ratio until both show the same \(B\), usually a common multiple of the two \(B\) values. The moment those numbers line up the three quantities click together into one longer ratio \(A:B:C\). The shared quantity is the hinge, and once everything hangs on it you can read off any comparison you want, even \(A:C\), which neither ratio showed you directly.

<svg viewBox="0 0 440 372" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A linking diagram that joins two ratios through their shared middle part. On the left, two short stacked ratio bars are drawn at different scales so the reader can see their middle parts do not match. The top bar shows front to middle equal to 4 to 3, with a front segment of length 4 and a middle segment of length 3. The bottom bar shows middle to rear equal to 2 to 5, with a middle segment of length 2 and a rear segment of length 5. Because the two middle parts, 3 and 2, are different lengths, the bars cannot be joined yet. An arrow labeled times 2 rescales the top ratio 4 to 3 into 8 to 6, and an arrow labeled times 3 rescales the bottom ratio 2 to 5 into 6 to 15. Now both middle parts equal 6. On the right, the two rescaled bars snap together at the shared middle of 6 to form one combined bar reading 8, then 6, then 15, that is front to middle to rear equal to 8 to 6 to 15. A bracket spans across the front end and the rear end of the combined bar and reads off front to rear equal to 8 to 15. A caption notes that matching the shared part is what lets two ratios join into one.">

blocks.cap: Two ratios that share a quantity, \(4:3\) for front to middle and \(2:5\) for middle to rear, do not line up while their shared part is written as \(3\) in one and \(2\) in the other. Scaling the first by \(2\) and the second by \(3\) turns both middles into \(6\), so the bars snap together into \(8:6:15\) and you can read off \(8:15\) for front to rear. Matching the shared part is the whole trick that lets two ratios join into one.

blocks.x: Yellow to blue is \(3:2\) and blue to white is \(4:7\). Rescale so the blue numbers agree, then write the ratio of yellow to white as a:b in lowest terms.
hints: Blue is \(2\) in the first ratio and \(4\) in the second. To make them match, double everything in the first ratio so its blue becomes \(4\). What does \(3:2\) become when you double both parts?

After doubling, yellow to blue is \(6:4\) and blue to white is \(4:7\). The blue \(4\) is now the same in both, so stack them into one chain \(6:4:7\). Read off just the yellow and the white parts, then reduce.
blocks.sol: The shared color is blue, but it carries different numbers in the two recipes, so first make those numbers agree. Blue is \(2\) in the yellow to blue ratio and \(4\) in the blue to white ratio. Doubling the first ratio turns its blue into \(4\) without changing the comparison, since multiplying both parts by the same number gives an equivalent ratio. So \(3:2\) becomes \(6:4\). The second ratio already has blue as \(4\), so leave it as \(4:7\). Now both recipes agree that blue is \(4\), and you can line all three colors up in one chain as yellow to blue to white, \(6:4:7\). Reading off only the first and last parts gives yellow to white as \(6:7\). The greatest common factor of \(6\) and \(7\) is \(1\), so this is already in lowest terms. The ratio of yellow to white is \(\boxed{6:7}\).

blocks.x: A major chord\'s notes vibrate near \(4:5:6\). The root-to-fifth gives \(4:6=3:2\), the "perfect fifth." Since 4, 5, 6 share no common factor, \(4:5:6\) is already in lowest terms.
A sourdough recipe: flour to water is \(5:3\), water to starter is \(6:5\). Water appears as 3 in one and 6 in the other. Rescale and combine. How many grams of flour are in a 630-gram batch?
hints: The water amount is 3 in the first ratio and 6 in the second. Rescale one ratio so both read the same number for water. Doubling \(5:3\) gives \(10:6\), which now matches the \(6\) in the water to starter ratio, so you can stitch them into one flour to water to starter ratio.
Once the three-way ratio is \(10:6:5\), add the parts to see how many equal shares the \(630\) grams splits into, find what one share weighs, then take the flour parts.
blocks.sol: The two ratios share water, but they disagree on its number. Flour to water is \(5:3\) and water to starter is \(6:5\). To link them, rescale the first ratio so its water matches the \(6\) in the second. Multiplying \(5:3\) by \(2\) gives \(10:6\), so flour to water is \(10:6\) and water to starter is \(6:5\). Now both call water \(6\), and the three line up into one ratio, \(10:6:5\). The greatest common factor of \(10\), \(6\), and \(5\) is \(1\), so it is already in lowest terms. Add the parts to get \(10+6+5=21\) equal shares. The batch weighs \(630\) grams, so one share weighs \(\tfrac{630}{21}=30\) grams. Flour is \(10\) shares, which is \(10\times 30 = 300\) grams. As a check, water is \(6\times 30 = 180\) and starter is \(5\times 30 = 150\), and \(300+180+150 = 630\). So the batch contains \(\boxed{300}\) grams of flour.

review.x: A birdseed recipe follows the ratio \(7:4:2\), millet to sunflower to cracked corn. Using exactly one unscaled batch, how many parts of birdseed are there in total?
hints: A multi-way ratio like \(7:4:2\) hides a total inside it. The whole blend is made of all of its parts added together.
Add the three part numbers, \(7\), \(4\), and \(2\). One unscaled batch means you do not multiply any of them first.
review.sol: A multi-way ratio carries a hidden total, the sum of all its parts. Here the blend is \(7\) parts millet, \(4\) parts sunflower, and \(2\) parts cracked corn. One full unscaled batch uses those numbers exactly as written, so no rescaling is needed and each part keeps its given size. Add every part to find the whole, \(7 + 4 + 2 = 13\). So one batch is \(\boxed{13}\) parts of birdseed in total.

review.x: A printmaker logs a blend as \(20:50:35\), cyan to magenta to yellow. Divide every part by the GCF of all three. Write the simplified ratio in the form a:b:c.
hints: To simplify a multi-way ratio you divide every part by the same number, the greatest common factor of all three parts. List the factors of \(20\), \(50\), and \(35\) and find the largest one they all share.

All three parts end in \(0\) or \(5\), so they are all multiples of \(5\). Check whether anything bigger than \(5\) divides all three. It does not, since \(35\) is not even, so \(5\) is the greatest common factor. Divide each part by \(5\).
review.sol: First find the greatest common factor of all three parts. The factors of \(20\) are \(1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20\), the factors of \(50\) are \(1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50\), and the factors of \(35\) are \(1, 5, 7, 35\). The largest number on all three lists is \(5\), so the greatest common factor is \(5\). Now divide every part by \(5\). You get \(20 \div 5 = 4\), then \(50 \div 5 = 10\), then \(35 \div 5 = 7\). That gives \(4:10:7\). Check that this is fully reduced. The parts are \(4\), \(10\), and \(7\), and \(7\) is prime and does not divide \(4\) or \(10\), so the three parts share no common factor larger than \(1\). The blend in lowest terms is \(\boxed{4:10:7}\).

review.x: A trail mix uses oats, raisins, and seeds in the ratio \(10:9:25\). Reduce to lowest terms, if nothing divides all three, the original is the answer. Write your answer as a:b:c.
hints: To reduce a multi-way ratio you need the greatest common factor of all three parts at once, not just two of them. A number only counts if it divides \(10\), \(9\), and \(25\) without leaving a remainder. Notice that \(9\) is odd, so \(2\) is out, and \(9\) is not a multiple of \(5\), so \(5\) is out too.

Check the factors of \(9\), which are \(1\), \(3\), and \(9\). Does \(3\) divide \(10\)? Does \(9\) divide \(10\)? If the only number that divides all three parts is \(1\), the ratio is already in lowest terms and you leave it exactly as it is.
review.sol: To reduce a multi-way ratio you divide every part by the greatest common factor of all three numbers together, so the real task is to find what \(10\), \(9\), and \(25\) share. Look at \(9\) first, since it is the most restrictive. It is odd, so \(2\) cannot divide it, which already rules out any even common factor. Its only factors are \(1\), \(3\), and \(9\). Now test \(3\) against the others. It does not divide \(10\), since \(10=3\times 3+1\), so \(3\) is not a common factor. The same goes for \(9\). That leaves only \(1\) as a number dividing all three parts, so the greatest common factor of \(10\), \(9\), and \(25\) is \(1\). When the greatest common factor is \(1\), there is nothing to divide out, and the ratio is already as small as it can get. Dividing each part by \(1\) just gives back \(10:9:25\), so the comparison was already in lowest terms from the start. \(\boxed{10:9:25}\)

review.x: A flower farmer counts 42 marigolds, 14 basil plants, and 28 thyme plants. Simplify the ratio \(42:14:28\) to lowest terms. Write as a:b:c.
hints: A multi-way ratio is in lowest terms when the greatest common factor of all three parts is \(1\). Start by listing the factors of each part and finding the largest number that divides \(42\), \(14\), and \(28\) all at once.
Each part is even and each is a multiple of \(7\), so try dividing every part by \(14\). Keep the order the same: do \(42\div 14\), then \(14\div 14\), then \(28\div 14\).
review.sol: To reduce a multi-way ratio I divide every part by the greatest common factor of all the parts, not just two of them. The parts are \(42\), \(14\), and \(28\). The factors of \(14\) are \(1, 2, 7, 14\), and the largest of these that also divides \(42\) and \(28\) is \(14\) itself, since \(42=14\times 3\) and \(28=14\times 2\). So the greatest common factor of all three is \(14\). Now I divide each part by \(14\), keeping the order marigolds, basil, thyme. That gives \(42\div 14=3\), then \(14\div 14=1\), then \(28\div 14=2\), so the ratio becomes \(3:1:2\). I can check by scaling back up, since \(3\times 14:1\times 14:2\times 14=42:14:28\), the original. The new parts \(3\), \(1\), and \(2\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), so this is fully reduced. \(\boxed{3:1:2}\)

review.x: A smoothie uses \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) cup mango, \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) cup spinach, and \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) cup yogurt. Clear fractions with a common denominator and reduce. Write the ratio as a:b:c.
hints: The three denominators are 3, 6, and 2. Find one number that all of them divide into evenly, then multiply every part by it so the fractions disappear.
The smallest number that 3, 6, and 2 all divide into is 6. Multiply each cup amount by 6 to get whole numbers, then check whether those whole numbers share a common factor you can divide out.
review.sol: To compare these fairly, the cup amounts need to be whole numbers. The denominators are 3, 6, and 2, and the smallest number all three divide into evenly is 6. Multiply every part by 6. Mango becomes \(\tfrac{1}{3}\times 6 = 2\), spinach becomes \(\tfrac{1}{6}\times 6 = 1\), and yogurt becomes \(\tfrac{1}{2}\times 6 = 3\). Multiplying all three parts by the same number keeps the ratio equivalent, so \(\tfrac{1}{3} : \tfrac{1}{6} : \tfrac{1}{2}\) is the same as \(2:1:3\). Now check for a common factor across all three parts. The greatest common factor of 2, 1, and 3 is 1, so the ratio is already in lowest terms. The simplified ratio of mango to spinach to yogurt is \(\boxed{2:1:3}\).

review.x: A candle recipe uses \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\) oz soy wax, \(1\tfrac{1}{4}\) oz beeswax, and \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) oz fragrance oil. Clear fractions, then reduce. Write the ratio as a:b:c in lowest terms.
hints: Every part is a number of eighths. Multiply each part by 8 to clear all the fractions at once, and you get three whole numbers.
After scaling you have \(20:10:5\). Find the greatest common factor of all three parts and divide each part by it.
review.sol: Start with the three amounts \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\), \(1\tfrac{1}{4}\), and \(\tfrac{5}{8}\). The smallest denominator that fits all of them is 8, so scaling every part up by 8 will clear the fractions while keeping the ratio the same. Soy wax becomes \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\times 8 = 20\), beeswax becomes \(1\tfrac{1}{4}\times 8 = 10\), and fragrance oil becomes \(\tfrac{5}{8}\times 8 = 5\). That gives the whole-number ratio \(20:10:5\). Now divide out the common factor. The greatest common factor of \(20\), \(10\), and \(5\) is \(5\), so divide each part by \(5\) to get \(4:2:1\). Since the parts \(4\), \(2\), and \(1\) share no factor bigger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. The ratio of soy wax to beeswax to fragrance oil is \(\boxed{4:2:1}\).

review.x: A mosaic border of 96 tiles uses gold, copper, and slate in the ratio \(1:2:5\). How many copper tiles are in the border?
hints: Add the three numbers in the ratio to count the parts. There are \(1+2+5=8\) equal parts hidden in those \(96\) tiles, so first work out how many tiles sit in one part.
One part is \(96\div 8=12\) tiles. Copper is the middle number, so it takes up \(2\) of those parts. Multiply to finish.
review.sol: The ratio \(1:2:5\) splits the border into \(1+2+5=8\) equal parts, and all \(96\) tiles fill those \(8\) parts evenly. One part is worth $$96\div 8=12\text{ tiles}.$$ Copper matches the middle number in the ratio, which is \(2\) parts, so the copper count is \(2\times 12=24\). As a quick check, gold takes \(1\times 12=12\) tiles and slate takes \(5\times 12=60\) tiles, and \(12+24+60=96\), which matches the border. So the number of copper tiles is \(\boxed{24}\).

review.x: A 75-bead bracelet uses jade, amber, onyx, and pearl in the ratio \(2:4:3:6\). How many pearl beads are on the bracelet?
hints: Add all four parts of \(2:4:3:6\) to find how many equal shares the bracelet splits into, then divide \(75\) by that count to learn what a single share is worth.
One share is \(75 \div 15 = 5\) beads. Pearl carries \(6\) of those shares, so multiply \(5\) by \(6\).
review.sol: The comparison \(2:4:3:6\) tells you how the \(75\) beads divide into equal shares. Add every part to count the shares, \(2+4+3+6 = 15\), so the whole bracelet is built from \(15\) equal shares. Find what one share is worth by splitting the total evenly, \(75 \div 15 = 5\), so each single share is \(5\) beads. Now scale up to the kind the question asks about. Pearl holds \(6\) shares, so the pearl count is \(6 \times 5 = 30\). As a check, jade gives \(2 \times 5 = 10\), amber gives \(4 \times 5 = 20\), and onyx gives \(3 \times 5 = 15\), and \(10 + 20 + 15 + 30 = 75\), which matches the bracelet exactly. So the bracelet carries \(\boxed{30}\) pearl beads.

review.x: A robotics club has builders, coders, and designers in the ratio \(8:5:3\). Tonight 35 coders showed up. How many builders does the club have?
hints: The coder share is the middle number, \(5\) parts. If those \(5\) parts equal \(35\) coders, what is one part worth?
Builders are the first number, \(8\) parts. Multiply the value of one part by \(8\).
review.sol: The comparison \(8:5:3\) means builders take \(8\) parts, coders take \(5\) parts, and designers take \(3\) parts, all the same size. The coders are the link here, so start there. There are \(35\) coders sitting in \(5\) parts, so one part is worth \(35 \div 5 = 7\) members. Now rescale to builders. Builders take \(8\) parts, and each part is worth \(7\), so the club has \(8 \times 7 = 56\) builders. As a quick check, designers would be \(3 \times 7 = 21\), and \(56 + 35 + 21 = 112\) members in total, which is \(16\) equal parts of \(7\). The number of builders is \(\boxed{56}\).

review.x: A produce crate is packed plums to apples to pears in the ratio \(2:9:4\). The crate holds 28 pears. How many apples are in the crate?
hints: The pears sit in the last slot of \(2:9:4\), so they take 4 equal parts. If those 4 parts together make 28 pears, what is one single part worth?
Once you know one part is \(28 \div 4 = 7\) pears, the apples take 9 of those same parts. Multiply \(9 \times 7\).
review.sol: In the comparison \(2:9:4\) the pears are the third number, so they are worth 4 parts. The crate holds 28 pears, and those 28 pears fill 4 equal parts, so one part is \(28 \div 4 = 7\) pieces of fruit. Now look at the apples. They are the middle number, worth 9 parts. Each part is 7, so the apples come to \(9 \times 7 = 63\). That gives \(\boxed{63}\).

review.x: A relay team splits training as run to bike to swim to row in the ratio \(6:5:4:3\). The bike leg is 20 km. How many kilometers is the whole training distance?
hints: The bike leg is worth \(5\) parts and equals \(20\) kilometers. One part is \(20 \div 5\).
Add the parts \(6+5+4+3\) to see how many parts the whole distance is, then multiply that by what one part is worth.
review.sol: The comparison \(6:5:4:3\) is already in lowest terms, since the only number that divides all four parts is \(1\). The bike leg sits at the \(5\) in that comparison, so it is worth \(5\) parts, and you are told those \(5\) parts equal \(20\) kilometers. Rescale to find one part. One part is \(20 \div 5 = 4\) kilometers. The whole distance covers every part, so add them up, \(6+5+4+3 = 18\) parts. Multiply to get the total, \(18 \times 4 = 72\) kilometers. So the whole training distance is \(\boxed{72}\) kilometers.

review.x: A spice jar blends cumin, paprika, and chili in the ratio \(7:2:1\). The jar has 18 grams of paprika. How many grams of spice are in the jar altogether?
hints: Paprika sits at 2 parts in the ratio, and those 2 parts are worth 18 grams. Split that to find what a single part weighs.
Once you know one part, add up the parts. The whole jar is \(7+2+1\) parts, so multiply the value of one part by that total.
review.sol: Paprika is the middle number in \(7:2:1\), so it stands for 2 parts. Those 2 parts weigh 18 grams, so one part is \(18\div 2 = 9\) grams. The whole jar is \(7+2+1 = 10\) parts. Multiply the value of one part by the total number of parts to rescale up to the full jar, \(10\times 9 = 90\) grams. As a check, cumin is \(7\times 9 = 63\) grams, paprika is \(2\times 9 = 18\) grams, and chili is \(1\times 9 = 9\) grams, and \(63+18+9 = 90\). \(\boxed{90}\)

review.x: A clockwork toy has three gears. Gear 1 to gear 2 turns at \(5:6\); gear 2 to gear 3 turns at \(4:3\). Rescale so the gear-2 numbers match, then write gear 1 to gear 3 as a:b in lowest terms.
hints: The second gear is the link between both ratios. Make its number the same in each one. It shows up as \(6\) and as \(4\), so find the smallest number both \(6\) and \(4\) divide into, which is \(12\).
Scale \(5:6\) up to \(10:12\) and scale \(4:3\) up to \(12:9\). Now the second gear reads \(12\) in both, so the full chain is \(10:12:9\). Read off only the first and third numbers.
review.sol: The second gear is the shared quantity, so the first job is to make its number match in both ratios. It appears as \(6\) in \(5:6\) and as \(4\) in \(4:3\). The smallest number that both \(6\) and \(4\) divide into is \(12\). Rescale the first ratio by multiplying both parts by \(2\), which gives \(5:6 = 10:12\). Rescale the second ratio by multiplying both parts by \(3\), which gives \(4:3 = 12:9\). Now the second gear reads \(12\) in each, so the three gears line up as \(10:12:9\). The first gear is \(10\) and the third gear is \(9\), so their comparison is \(10:9\). The greatest common factor of \(10\) and \(9\) is \(1\), so it is already in lowest terms. \(\boxed{10:9}\)

review.x: Nitrogen to phosphorus is \(2:3\) and phosphorus to potash is \(9:8\). Rescale the first ratio so its phosphorus matches 9, combine, then write nitrogen to potash as a:b in lowest terms.
hints: Phosphorus is the link. It reads \(3\) in the first ratio and \(9\) in the second, so multiply the whole first ratio by something to turn that \(3\) into a \(9\).
Once the phosphorus parts both equal \(9\), write one combined ratio nitrogen to phosphorus to potash, then ignore the phosphorus part and compare just nitrogen to potash.
review.sol: The two ratios share phosphorus, but they measure it with different numbers, so the first job is to make those numbers agree. In \(2:3\) the phosphorus part is \(3\), and in \(9:8\) the phosphorus part is \(9\). Multiply every part of the first ratio by \(3\) to lift that \(3\) up to \(9\). That turns nitrogen to phosphorus from \(2:3\) into \(6:9\). Now both ratios speak about phosphorus as \(9\), so they snap together into a single chain, nitrogen to phosphorus to potash equals \(6:9:8\). The question only asks how nitrogen compares to potash, so pull out those two parts, \(6:8\). The greatest common factor of \(6\) and \(8\) is \(2\), and dividing both by \(2\) gives \(\boxed{3:4}\).

blocks.x: A rate compares two quantities in different units, like dollars per pound or miles per hour. The word per signals a rate. A unit rate is the per-one value, how much of the first thing goes with exactly one unit of the second.
Dried cherries cost 20 dollars for 4 pounds. What is the price per pound, in dollars?
hints: The sign packs four pounds together into one price. To find what just one pound costs, share the \(20\) dollars equally among the \(4\) pounds.
Divide the dollars by the pounds, \(\tfrac{20 \text{ dollars}}{4 \text{ pounds}}\). That single quotient is the price for one pound.
blocks.sol: Four pounds together cost \(20\) dollars, and the cherries all sell at the same rate, so to find the price of one pound I share those dollars equally across the pounds by dividing. That gives \(\tfrac{20 \text{ dollars}}{4 \text{ pounds}} = 5\) dollars per pound. So one pound costs \(\boxed{5}\) dollars.

blocks.x: A reader finishes a 426-page novel in 6 days at a steady pace. How many pages per day did she read?
hints: A unit rate is the amount that goes with one of the second quantity. Here the second quantity is days, so you want the pages that go with one day. That means sharing all the pages equally among the 6 days.
Divide the total pages by the number of days. Compute \(426 \div 6\), and the result is the pages she reads each day.
blocks.sol: A rate compares two quantities in different units, and the unit rate is the amount per one of the second quantity. The second quantity is days, so divide the pages by the days. $$\frac{426 \text{ pages}}{6 \text{ days}} = 71 \text{ pages per day}.$$ You can check it by going the other way, since \(71 \text{ pages per day} \times 6 \text{ days} = 426\) pages, the whole novel. So she read \(\boxed{71}\) pages per day.

blocks.x: Rate vs. unit rate. A rate compares two quantities measured in different units, not the same-kind comparison from 7.1 and 7.2, and the word "per" is the giveaway, dollars per pound, pages per day, miles per hour. The unit rate is the per-one version, the amount of the first quantity that rides with a single unit of the second, and you find it by dividing the first quantity by the second. $$\tfrac{20\,\text{dollars}}{4\,\text{pounds}} = 5\text{ dollars per pound}$$ That is what makes a unit rate so handy, it strips a messy comparison down to one clean number.
Two olive-oil bottles: 15 oz for \$6.00, or 24 oz for \$8.40. Find the price per ounce of each and identify the better buy. What is the price per ounce of the better buy, in dollars?
hints: A unit rate is a cost for one of something. For each bottle, divide the total dollars by the number of ounces to get dollars for one ounce. Do this twice, once per bottle.
First bottle: \(\tfrac{\$6.00}{15 \text{ oz}} = \$0.40\) per ounce. Second bottle: \(\tfrac{\$8.40}{24 \text{ oz}} = \$0.35\) per ounce. The better buy is the smaller of these two numbers.
blocks.sol: Find the price per ounce of each bottle by dividing dollars by ounces. First bottle: $$\frac{\$6.00}{15 \text{ oz}} = \$0.40 \text{ per ounce}.$$ Second bottle: $$\frac{\$8.40}{24 \text{ oz}} = \$0.35 \text{ per ounce}.$$ Now compare the two unit rates. The second bottle costs \(\$0.35\) for each ounce while the first costs \(\$0.40\) for each ounce, so the second bottle is the better buy because you pay less for the same amount of oil. The price per ounce of the better buy is \(\boxed{0.35}\) dollars.

blocks.x: Best buy via unit rate. When two deals come in different sizes at different prices, you cannot rank them at a glance, since a bigger price might only mean a bigger package. Divide each price by its quantity to find what one single unit costs, one ounce or one pound or one anything. Once both are written as a price per one, the comparison is instant, and the better buy is whichever has the lower price per unit. The same per-one move ranks miles per gallon, words per minute, or anything measured this way.

Bottle A works out to \(\tfrac{\$6.00}{15 \text{ oz}} = 0.40\) dollars per ounce, while bottle B is \(\tfrac{\$8.40}{24 \text{ oz}} = 0.35\) dollars per ounce. On the shared baseline \(0.35\) sits below \(0.40\), so even though bottle B costs more in total, its lower price per unit makes it the better buy.

blocks.x: A drive covers 195 miles in 3 hours at a steady pace. What is the speed in miles per hour?
hints: The unit miles per hour is a recipe. It says take the miles and divide by the hours, which lands you on the miles covered in one hour.
Compute \(195 \div 3\). That tells you how many miles fit into each single hour of driving.
blocks.sol: Speed is distance divided by time, and the unit miles per hour spells out exactly that division. Put the miles on top and the hours on the bottom, then divide. $$\frac{195 \text{ miles}}{3 \text{ hours}} = 65 \text{ miles per hour}$$ So in every single hour the car covers 65 miles, which is the meaning of the "per one hour". The speed is \(\boxed{65}\) miles per hour.

blocks.x: A van cruises at 48 miles per hour for 7 hours straight. How many miles did it travel?
hints: Speed tells you the miles gained in one hour. To get the miles for all the hours, you do not divide here, you build up, so distance is speed times time.
Multiply the \(48\) miles in each hour by the \(7\) hours the van kept driving.
blocks.sol: Speed is distance per hour, so to recover the distance you multiply the speed by the number of hours. That gives \(\text{distance} = \text{speed} \times \text{time}\). Plug in the steady pace and the driving time, \(48 \tfrac{\text{miles}}{\text{hour}} \times 7 \text{ hours} = 336 \text{ miles}\). The "hours" in the rate cancels the "hours" of driving, leaving plain miles, which is exactly the unit the question wants. The van covered \(\boxed{336}\) miles.

blocks.x: Speed is distance ÷ time. Speed is just the rate of distance to time, and its unit label is an instruction in disguise, the thing before "per" is what you measured and the thing after it is what you divide by. So "miles per hour" means exactly \(\tfrac{\text{miles}}{\text{hours}}\). Rearranged, distance \(= \text{speed} \times \text{time}\) and time \(= \tfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}}\). Because a speed in miles per hour is already a per-one-hour amount, knowing any two of the three gives you the third.
An e-bike can hold 56 mph on a 392-mile route. How many hours does the ride take?
hints: Speed connects distance, time, and miles per hour. You have the distance and the speed, so flip the relationship around. Time is distance divided by speed.
Each hour covers 56 miles, so count how many 56 mile chunks fit in 392 miles. Compute \(392 \div 56\).
blocks.sol: Speed says distance divided by time, so to get the time you rearrange it the other way and use time equals distance divided by speed. Drop in the numbers, \(\tfrac{392 \text{ miles}}{56 \text{ miles per hour}} = 7 \text{ hours}\). You can check it by going forward, since \(56 \text{ miles per hour} \times 7 \text{ hours} = 392 \text{ miles}\), which matches the route exactly. The ride takes \(\boxed{7}\) hours.

blocks.x: The speed triangle. Speed, distance, and time are three names for one fact that rearranges to give whichever name is missing. $$\text{speed} = \tfrac{d}{t}, \qquad d = s \times t, \qquad t = \tfrac{d}{s}$$ A handy trick is to cover the quantity you are missing and look at the other two. If the missing one sits on top, multiply the two below it. If it sits under the line, divide. As soon as you know any two of the three, the last one drops right out.
