blocks.cap: When \(1 \text{ in} = 2.5 \text{ cm}\), a square that is one inch on each side has both its width and its height worth \(2.5\) cm. Its area is \(2.5 \text{ cm} \times 2.5 \text{ cm}\), the very same linear factor \(2.5\) used once across and once up, so it appears twice and effectively squares, giving \(2.5^2 = 6.25 \text{ sq cm}\), exactly the \(6.25\) little square-centimeter cells you can count inside. That is the area rule in one picture, since converting an area applies the linear factor twice, the conversion factor itself gets squared.

blocks.x: Area conversion squares the linear factor. An area is a length times a length, so when you convert it each length brings its own conversion factor, and using the factor twice is the same as squaring it. So if \(1\,\text{unit} = k\) of another, then \(1\,\text{square unit} = k^2\) square units, not just \(k\). The classic slip is squaring the number but forgetting the units do the same thing. Only multiplying the factor by itself cancels both copies of the unwanted unit and leaves a real square unit behind.
On a game island: \(1\,\text{brindle} = 5\,\text{floons}\), \(1\,\text{floon} = 4\,\text{ticks}\), \(1\,\text{tick} = 6\,\text{motes}\). A path is 3 brindles long. How many motes?
hints: You have no idea how big a brindle or a mote really is, and that is fine. The method runs on the relationships alone. Start with \(3 \text{ brindle}\) and pick the first conversion factor so that brindle cancels, which means brindle goes on the bottom, \(\frac{5 \text{ floons}}{1 \text{ brindle}}\).

Keep going one unit at a time, floon to tick to mote, each new factor oriented so the unit you just made lands on the bottom and cancels. After every cancellation only motes survive, so multiply the top numbers, \(3 \times 5 \times 4 \times 6\).
blocks.sol: Strange names, same machine. Lay the three given facts down as conversion factors, each a ratio equal to 1, and orient each one so the unwanted unit sits on the bottom and cancels. Brindle goes to floon, floon to tick, tick to mote. $$3 \text{ brindle} \times \frac{5 \text{ floons}}{1 \text{ brindle}} \times \frac{4 \text{ ticks}}{1 \text{ floon}} \times \frac{6 \text{ motes}}{1 \text{ tick}}$$ Watch the units fall away in order. The brindle on top cancels the brindle on the bottom, then floon cancels floon, then tick cancels tick. $$3 \cancel{\text{ brindle}} \times \frac{5 \cancel{\text{ floons}}}{1 \cancel{\text{ brindle}}} \times \frac{4 \cancel{\text{ ticks}}}{1 \cancel{\text{ floon}}} \times \frac{6 \text{ motes}}{1 \cancel{\text{ tick}}}$$ Only motes are left standing, exactly the unit the rulebook asked for. Now just multiply the numbers across the top, \(3 \times 5 \times 4 \times 6\). $$3 \times 5 = 15, \quad 15 \times 4 = 60, \quad 60 \times 6 = 360$$ The path is \(\boxed{360}\) motes long. You never needed to picture a single one of these units. Give the method true relationships and a target, and it does the rest.

blocks.x: The method is unit-agnostic. Every conversion you have done, the single ones, the long chains, money and speeds, densities, areas, even invented units, is the exact same move. Multiply by a fraction that secretly equals \(1\), with its top and bottom the same real amount written two ways, aimed so the unwanted unit sits on the bottom and cancels. Because the fraction is really \(1\), the amount never changes, only its costume does, and the units are your safety check the whole way, since if they cancel down to the right label the chain is correct.

review.x: In the highlands, 1 league equals 3 miles. The Ridgeback Trail is 8 leagues long. How many miles long is the trail?
hints: You want leagues to disappear and miles to stay. Build the conversion factor \(\frac{3 \text{ miles}}{1 \text{ league}}\) so the league on top cancels the league in your starting amount.
Multiply \(8 \text{ leagues}\) by that factor. The word "leagues" cancels and you are left with miles, so just multiply the numbers.
review.sol: Start with the amount you have and multiply by a conversion factor that cancels leagues. Since 1 league equals 3 miles, the factor \(\frac{3 \text{ miles}}{1 \text{ league}}\) is just a fancy way of writing 1, and it puts leagues on the bottom so it cancels. $$8 \text{ leagues} \times \frac{3 \text{ miles}}{1 \text{ league}} = 8 \times \frac{3 \text{ miles}}{1} = 24 \text{ miles}$$ The \(\text{leagues}\) on top and bottom cancel, leaving miles, so the trail is \(\boxed{24}\) miles.

review.x: One gross equals 144 pencils. A warehouse has 360 loose pencils. How many gross is that?
hints: You want gross on top at the end, so orient the conversion factor as \(\frac{1 \text{ gross}}{144 \text{ pencils}}\). Multiply \(360 \text{ pencils}\) by it and watch the word pencils cancel.
After the units cancel you are left with the fraction \(\frac{360}{144}\). Reduce it to lowest terms by dividing the top and bottom by their greatest common factor, \(72\).
review.sol: Start with the \(360\) pencils and choose the conversion factor that puts gross on top so pencils cancels. $$360 \text{ pencils} \times \frac{1 \text{ gross}}{144 \text{ pencils}} = \frac{360}{144} \text{ gross}$$ The word pencils sits on the top of one piece and the bottom of the other, so it cancels and leaves gross. Now reduce the leftover number. Both \(360\) and \(144\) share the factor \(72\), and \(360 \div 72 = 5\) while \(144 \div 72 = 2\). $$\frac{360}{144} = \frac{5}{2}$$ So the warehouse has \(\boxed{5/2}\) gross of pencils, which is two and a half gross.

review.x: One ream equals 500 sheets. A dock has 3,500 sheets. Using the factor \(\frac{1\,\text{ream}}{500\,\text{sheets}}\), how many reams is that?
hints: You want sheets on top to cancel sheets, so the conversion factor should have sheets on the bottom. That points you to \(\frac{1 \text{ ream}}{500 \text{ sheets}}\).
Multiply \(3{,}500 \text{ sheets}\) by that factor. The word sheets cancels, and you are left with \(\frac{3{,}500}{500}\) reams.
review.sol: Start with the amount you have and multiply by the conversion factor oriented so sheets cancels. $$3{,}500 \text{ sheets} \times \frac{1 \text{ ream}}{500 \text{ sheets}} = \frac{3{,}500}{500} \text{ reams}$$ The unit sheets appears on top and bottom, so it cancels and reams remains. Now divide. $$\frac{3{,}500}{500} = 7$$ So \(3{,}500\) sheets is \(\boxed{7}\) reams.

review.x: Each brick is 9 inches long. A wall is 162 inches long. Using the factor \(\frac{1\,\text{brick}}{9\,\text{in}}\) so the inches cancel, how many bricks long is the wall?
hints: You want inches to cancel and bricks to remain. Put inches on the bottom of the factor, so write \(162 \text{ in} \times \frac{1 \text{ brick}}{9 \text{ in}}\) and watch the inches disappear.
After the inches cancel you are left with \(\frac{162}{9}\) bricks. Divide \(162\) by \(9\).
review.sol: Start with the wall length and attach the conversion factor with inches on the bottom, so the inches cancel and bricks are left. $$162 \text{ in} \times \frac{1 \text{ brick}}{9 \text{ in}}$$ The inch units cancel, top and bottom. $$162 \cancel{\text{ in}} \times \frac{1 \text{ brick}}{9 \cancel{\text{ in}}} = \frac{162}{9} \text{ bricks}$$ Now divide. $$\frac{162}{9} = 18 \text{ bricks}$$ So the wall is \(\boxed{18}\) bricks long.

review.x: A surveyor has \(1\,\text{mile} = 8\,\text{furlongs}\) and \(1\,\text{furlong} = 40\,\text{rods}\). A boundary is 2 miles. Chain two factors and convert to rods. How many rods long is the boundary?
hints: You are starting in miles and you want rods, but you have no direct mile to rod fact. Go through furlongs as a stepping stone, so first turn miles into furlongs, then turn those furlongs into rods.

Build a chain of two conversion factors. Orient \(\frac{8 \text{ furlongs}}{1 \text{ mile}}\) so the mile cancels, then orient \(\frac{40 \text{ rods}}{1 \text{ furlong}}\) so the furlong cancels, leaving only rods.
review.sol: Start from the quantity you have, \(2\) miles, and multiply by conversion factors that each equal \(1\), choosing the orientation that cancels the unit you want to get rid of. First cancel miles using \(\frac{8 \text{ furlongs}}{1 \text{ mile}}\), then cancel furlongs using \(\frac{40 \text{ rods}}{1 \text{ furlong}}\). $$2 \text{ mile} \times \frac{8 \text{ furlong}}{1 \text{ mile}} \times \frac{40 \text{ rod}}{1 \text{ furlong}}$$ The mile on top cancels the mile on the bottom, and the furlong on top cancels the furlong on the bottom, so only rods survive. Now multiply the numbers, \(2 \times 8 \times 40 = 640\). The boundary line is \(\boxed{640}\) rods long.

review.x: A recipe needs 5 gallons of water, measured in pints. Given \(1\,\text{gal} = 4\,\text{qt}\) and \(1\,\text{qt} = 2\,\text{pt}\), chain two factors. How many pints are in 5 gallons?
hints: You are starting in gallons and want to land in pints, but there is no direct gallon-to-pint fact given. Go through quarts as a stepping stone, first gallons to quarts, then quarts to pints.

Build each conversion factor so the unit you want to remove sits on the bottom. Use \(\frac{4 \text{ quart}}{1 \text{ gallon}}\) to cancel gallons, then \(\frac{2 \text{ pint}}{1 \text{ quart}}\) to cancel quarts.
review.sol: Start with \(5\) gallons and chain two conversion factors so the unwanted units cancel. Orient each factor with the unit you want to remove on the bottom. $$5 \text{ gallon} \times \frac{4 \text{ quart}}{1 \text{ gallon}} \times \frac{2 \text{ pint}}{1 \text{ quart}}$$ The gallons cancel against the gallon on the bottom of the first factor, and the quarts cancel against the quart on the bottom of the second factor, leaving only pints. $$5 \times 4 \times 2 = 40 \text{ pint}$$ So \(5\) gallons is \(\boxed{40}\) pints.

review.x: How many seconds are in 1 full day? Chain three factors using \(1\,\text{day} = 24\,\text{hr}\), \(1\,\text{hr} = 60\,\text{min}\), \(1\,\text{min} = 60\,\text{s}\).
hints: Stack the factors so each unwanted unit sits where it will cancel. Start with \(1 \text{ day} \times \frac{24 \text{ hours}}{1 \text{ day}}\) so day cancels, then multiply by a factor that cancels hours, then one that cancels minutes.
Once every unit but seconds has cancelled, you are just multiplying the three numbers on top. Work out \(24 \times 60 \times 60\).
review.sol: Write 1 day and multiply by three conversion factors, each turned so the unwanted unit is on the bottom and cancels. $$1 \text{ day} \times \frac{24 \text{ hours}}{1 \text{ day}} \times \frac{60 \text{ minutes}}{1 \text{ hour}} \times \frac{60 \text{ seconds}}{1 \text{ minute}}$$ The day cancels with the day in the first factor, the hour cancels with the hour in the second factor, and the minute cancels with the minute in the third factor, leaving only seconds. $$1 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60 \text{ seconds} = 86{,}400 \text{ seconds}$$ So one full day holds \(\boxed{86400}\) seconds.

review.x: A collection is 3 stacks. Each stack holds 5 bundles, each bundle holds 4 packs, each pack holds 12 cards. Chain three factors and convert stacks to cards. How many cards is that?
hints: You have to travel from stacks all the way down to cards, passing through bundles and packs on the way. Build one conversion factor for each step, and orient each fraction so the unit you are leaving sits on the bottom to cancel.

Start with \(3 \text{ stacks}\) and multiply by \(\frac{5 \text{ bundles}}{1 \text{ stack}}\), then \(\frac{4 \text{ packs}}{1 \text{ bundle}}\), then \(\frac{12 \text{ cards}}{1 \text{ pack}}\). Check that stacks, bundles, and packs each cancel before you multiply the numbers.
review.sol:
Chain three conversion factors, one per step, each oriented so the old unit cancels. Start from the 3 stacks and walk down to cards.
$$3 \text{ stacks} \times \frac{5 \text{ bundles}}{1 \text{ stack}} \times \frac{4 \text{ packs}}{1 \text{ bundle}} \times \frac{12 \text{ cards}}{1 \text{ pack}}$$
Watch the units cancel down the line. The \(\text{stacks}\) cancel against \(\text{stack}\), the \(\text{bundles}\) cancel against \(\text{bundle}\), and the \(\text{packs}\) cancel against \(\text{pack}\), leaving only \(\text{cards}\).
$$3 \times \cancel{\text{stacks}} \times \frac{5 \,\cancel{\text{bundles}}}{1 \,\cancel{\text{stack}}} \times \frac{4 \,\cancel{\text{packs}}}{1 \,\cancel{\text{bundle}}} \times \frac{12 \text{ cards}}{1 \,\cancel{\text{pack}}}$$
Now multiply the numbers. \(3 \times 5 = 15\), then \(15 \times 4 = 60\), then \(60 \times 12 = 720\). So the collection holds \(\boxed{720}\) cards.

review.x: A festival booth exchanges 1 euro for 12 ride tokens. A visitor exchanges 15 euros. Using the factor \(\frac{12\,\text{tokens}}{1\,\text{euro}}\), how many tokens does the visitor get?
hints: A conversion factor is just a ratio equal to 1. The booth tells you that 12 tokens and 1 euro are worth the same, so \(\frac{12 \text{ tokens}}{1 \text{ euro}}\) equals 1 and you can multiply by it without changing the value.

Write the 15 euros next to the factor and check which unit cancels. You want "euro" on top of your starting amount to cancel "euro" on the bottom of the factor, leaving tokens. Then multiply \(15 \times 12\).
review.sol: Start with the amount you have and multiply by the conversion factor, lined up so the unwanted unit cancels. $$15 \text{ euros} \times \frac{12 \text{ tokens}}{1 \text{ euro}}$$ The word "euro" appears on top of the starting amount and on the bottom of the factor, so it cancels. $$15 \cancel{\text{ euros}} \times \frac{12 \text{ tokens}}{1 \cancel{\text{ euro}}} = 15 \times 12 \text{ tokens}$$ That leaves only tokens, which is the target unit. Multiplying gives \(15 \times 12 = 180\) ride tokens. \(\boxed{180}\)

review.x: A summer fair: 1 dollar buys 20 game chips, and 1 chip earns 3 prize points. A guest exchanges 7 dollars. Chain two factors through chips. How many prize points is that worth?
hints: Build two conversion factors, each equal to 1. One turns dollars into chips, \(\frac{20 \text{ chips}}{1 \text{ dollar}}\), and one turns chips into points, \(\frac{3 \text{ points}}{1 \text{ chip}}\).
Start with \(7 \text{ dollars}\) and multiply by both factors so that dollars cancel first, then chips cancel, leaving only prize points.
review.sol: Treat each exchange as a conversion factor, a fraction equal to 1 because its top and bottom are the same value in two units. Orient each one so the unwanted unit sits on the bottom and cancels. Starting from \(7\) dollars, $$7 \text{ dollars} \times \frac{20 \text{ chips}}{1 \text{ dollar}} \times \frac{3 \text{ points}}{1 \text{ chip}}.$$ The dollars cancel against the first factor, and the chips cancel against the second, $$7 \cancel{\text{ dollars}} \times \frac{20 \cancel{\text{ chips}}}{1 \cancel{\text{ dollar}}} \times \frac{3 \text{ points}}{1 \cancel{\text{ chip}}} = 7 \times 20 \times 3 \text{ points}.$$ Multiplying gives \(7 \times 20 = 140\) chips along the way, then \(140 \times 3 = 420\) prize points. So \(7\) dollars is worth \(\boxed{420}\) prize points.

review.x: A maglev train travels at 72 km/hr. Convert to m/s using \(1\,\text{km} = 1{,}000\,\text{m}\) and \(1\,\text{hr} = 3{,}600\,\text{s}\). What is the speed in meters per second?
hints: Each conversion factor is a ratio equal to 1, so orient each one to cancel a unit you do not want. Put \(\text{km}\) on the bottom of the first factor to cancel the \(\text{km}\) on top, and put \(\text{hr}\) on top of the second factor to cancel the \(\text{hr}\) on the bottom.

After cancelling, you are left with meters on top and seconds on the bottom, which is exactly meters per second. Multiply the numbers across the top and across the bottom, then simplify \(\frac{72 \times 1{,}000}{3{,}600}\).
review.sol:
Build the chain so the unwanted units cancel one at a time. Start with the given speed and attach both conversion factors, each a ratio equal to 1.

$$\frac{72 \text{ km}}{1 \text{ hr}} \times \frac{1{,}000 \text{ m}}{1 \text{ km}} \times \frac{1 \text{ hr}}{3{,}600 \text{ s}}$$

The \(\text{km}\) on top cancels the \(\text{km}\) on the bottom of the second factor, and the \(\text{hr}\) on the bottom cancels the \(\text{hr}\) on top of the third factor.

$$\frac{72 \cancel{\text{ km}}}{1 \cancel{\text{ hr}}} \times \frac{1{,}000 \text{ m}}{1 \cancel{\text{ km}}} \times \frac{1 \cancel{\text{ hr}}}{3{,}600 \text{ s}}$$

What survives is meters on top and seconds on the bottom, which is meters per second. Now multiply the numbers.

$$\frac{72 \times 1{,}000}{3{,}600} \text{ m/s} = \frac{72{,}000}{3{,}600} \text{ m/s} = 20 \text{ m/s}$$

So the train moves at \(\boxed{20}\) meters per second.

review.x: A runner's pace is 15 mph. Convert to feet per minute using \(1\,\text{mi} = 5{,}280\,\text{ft}\) and \(1\,\text{hr} = 60\,\text{min}\). What is the speed in feet per minute?
hints: You need to change two units at once, miles into feet on top and hours into minutes on the bottom. Pick a conversion factor for each so that the unit you want to remove sits on the opposite side of a fraction bar from where it starts.

Write \(\frac{15 \text{ mi}}{1 \text{ hr}}\) and multiply by \(\frac{5{,}280 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ mi}}\) to clear the miles, then by \(\frac{1 \text{ hr}}{60 \text{ min}}\) to clear the hours. The miles and hours cancel and feet over minutes is all that survives.
review.sol: Start with the given rate and attach one conversion factor for each unit you want to change. To turn miles into feet, multiply by \(\frac{5{,}280 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ mi}}\), which puts miles on the bottom so it cancels the miles on top. To turn hours into minutes, multiply by \(\frac{1 \text{ hr}}{60 \text{ min}}\), which puts hours on top so it cancels the hours on the bottom. $$\frac{15 \text{ mi}}{1 \text{ hr}} \times \frac{5{,}280 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ mi}} \times \frac{1 \text{ hr}}{60 \text{ min}}$$ Cancel the matching units. $$\frac{15 \cancel{\text{ mi}}}{1 \cancel{\text{ hr}}} \times \frac{5{,}280 \text{ ft}}{1 \cancel{\text{ mi}}} \times \frac{1 \cancel{\text{ hr}}}{60 \text{ min}}$$ What is left is feet over minutes, exactly the unit we want. Now do the arithmetic. $$\frac{15 \times 5{,}280}{60} \text{ ft/min} = \frac{79{,}200}{60} \text{ ft/min} = 1{,}320 \text{ ft/min}$$ The runner is moving at \(\boxed{1320}\) feet per minute.

review.x: A jar holds 8 tablespoons of maple syrup. The density is \(\frac{21\,\text{g}}{1\,\text{tablespoon}}\). How many grams of syrup does the jar hold?
hints: The density \(\frac{21 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ tablespoon}}\) is your conversion factor. Set it up so the word "tablespoon" sits on the bottom, since you are starting with tablespoons and want them to cancel.
Multiply \(8 \text{ tablespoons} \times \frac{21 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ tablespoon}}\). The tablespoon units cancel and only grams are left, so all you have to do is the arithmetic \(8 \times 21\).
review.sol: Start with the amount you know and attach the density as a conversion factor, oriented so tablespoons cancels and grams stays. $$8 \text{ tablespoons} \times \frac{21 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ tablespoon}}$$ The \(\text{tablespoon}\) on top cancels with the \(\text{tablespoon}\) on the bottom. $$8 \, \cancel{\text{tablespoons}} \times \frac{21 \text{ g}}{1 \, \cancel{\text{tablespoon}}} = (8 \times 21) \text{ g}$$ Now finish the multiplication, \(8 \times 21 = 168\), and the only surviving unit is grams. So the jar holds \(\boxed{168}\) grams of syrup.

review.x: A workshop floor covers 6 square feet. Convert to square inches using \(1\,\text{ft} = 12\,\text{in}\). Remember the linear factor gets applied twice for area. What is the area in square inches?
hints: A foot of length equals 12 inches, so 1 square foot equals \(12 \times 12\) square inches because both the length and the width of the square get multiplied by 12.
Build the conversion factor \(\frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}\) and apply it twice, which is the same as squaring it, so the square feet cancel and square inches remain.
review.sol: Start with the area and multiply by the conversion factor \(\frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}\) twice, since area carries two lengths. $$6 \text{ ft}^2 \times \frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}} \times \frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}$$ Notice the \(\text{ft}^2\) on top cancels with the two \(\text{ft}\) on the bottom, leaving \(\text{in}^2\). $$6 \times 12 \times 12 \text{ in}^2 = 6 \times 144 \text{ in}^2 = 864 \text{ in}^2$$ So the floor is \(\boxed{864}\) square inches.
